gradeBasis: Meditations are his private notebooks, first-person throughout, the least performed voice in the ancient world.
oneLine: Marcus Aurelius, whose Meditations are his private notebooks, written to himself and never intended for publication - the least performed voice in the ancient world.

gradeBasis: Moral Letters to Lucilius are his own voice, 124 real letters of Stoic counsel, first person throughout.
edition: Richard Mott Gummere, Loeb 1917-1925, via en.wikisource.org
oneLine: Seneca, whose Moral Letters to Lucilius are 124 real letters of Stoic counsel written to his friend Lucilius, first person throughout.

gradeBasis: First-person voice preserved via his student Arrian - strong but one remove from the man.
edition: George Long translation, Project Gutenberg #45109 (The Enchiridion)
oneLine: Epictetus, voiced entirely from The Enchiridion, tr. George Long.

gradeBasis: First-person autobiography, his own voice, no traps.
oneLine: Frederick Douglass, voiced entirely from Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, 1845.

gradeBasis: Marriott edition includes his personal letters (e.g. the 1513 letter to Vettori).
oneLine: Machiavelli, voiced entirely from The Prince, tr. W. K. Marriott 1908.

gradeBasis: Full first-person memoir, dictated on his deathbed.
oneLine: Ulysses S. Grant, voiced entirely from Personal Memoirs of U. S. Grant, 1885.

gradeBasis: Deep first-person daily corpus.
oneLine: Thoreau, voiced entirely from Walden, 1854.

gradeBasis: Full first-person autobiography, pre-1930, clean.
oneLine: Theodore Roosevelt, voiced entirely from Theodore Roosevelt: An Autobiography, 1913.

gradeBasis: Full Essays, deeply first-person self-examination.
edition: Charles Cotton translation, Hazlitt revision, Project Gutenberg #3600
oneLine: Montaigne, voiced entirely from Essays, tr. Charles Cotton (Hazlitt revision).

gradeBasis: Richest first-person day-to-day corpus on the list.
edition: Henry B. Wheatley edition 1893-1899, Project Gutenberg #4200 (complete)
oneLine: Samuel Pepys, voiced entirely from Diary of Samuel Pepys, Wheatley edition 1893-1899.

gradeBasis: Confessions is first-person throughout.
edition: E. B. Pusey translation, Project Gutenberg #3296
licenseBasis: translation pre-1930, translator long dead, public domain
oneLine: Augustine, voiced entirely from The Confessions, tr. E. B. Pusey.

gradeBasis: First-person essays, his own voice, clean for the 1903 work specifically.
oneLine: W. E. B. Du Bois, voiced entirely from The Souls of Black Folk, 1903.

gradeBasis: Genuinely first-person recollections plus her actual correspondence, but compiled and edited posthumously by her daughter Martha - curated rather than raw, disclose (same shape as the Victoria discount, milder because the letters are primary).
oneLine: Mary Somerville, voiced entirely from Personal Recollections of Mary Somerville, 1873.

gradeBasis: Fictional-character corpus: the Count's own voice lives in dialogue inside the other characters' journals - the persona must disclose that the narration is not his.
licenseBasis: US copyright formalities failure at publication; public domain
oneLine: Count Dracula, voiced entirely from Dracula, Bram Stoker, 1897.

gradeBasis: The creature narrates his own story first-person in the middle volume - a real first-person core inside Walton's and Victor's frame narratives; disclose the frame.
edition: Project Gutenberg #84 (the commonly circulated 1831 revised text)
oneLine: Frankenstein's Creature, voiced entirely from Frankenstein, Mary Shelley (1831 text).

gradeBasis: Alice's voice is dialogue and reported thought within Carroll's third-person narration - disclose.
oneLine: Alice, voiced entirely from Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, 1865.

gradeBasis: Ahab's voice is dialogue and soliloquy inside Ishmael's first-person narration - the persona must disclose that Ishmael, not Ahab, tells the story.
oneLine: Captain Ahab, voiced entirely from Moby-Dick; or, The Whale, 1851.

gradeBasis: Elizabeth's voice is dialogue plus Austen's free indirect discourse within third-person narration - disclose.
oneLine: Elizabeth Bennet, voiced entirely from Pride and Prejudice, 1813.

gradeBasis: A with Butler SPECIFICALLY (plain readable prose, faithful enough); B if defaulting to Chapman or Pope, whose PD versions are loose adaptations.
edition: Samuel Butler prose translation 1900, Project Gutenberg #1727
oneLine: Odysseus, voiced entirely from The Odyssey, tr. Samuel Butler 1900.

gradeBasis: Gatsby is seen entirely through Nick Carraway's first-person narration; his own voice is dialogue only - disclose.
licenseBasis: 95-year US term from 1925 publication expired Jan 1 2021; public domain
oneLine: Jay Gatsby, voiced entirely from The Great Gatsby, 1925.
